item.title: Lzon.ca. A personal blog, by a programmer and IT expert.
item.description: A personal blog, by a programmer and IT expert. Essays, Articles, Guides, and Recipes. As well as Code, Quotes, and Links.

item.title: The Vercel Plugin on Claude Code wants to read all your prompts!
item.description: 09 Apr 2026 The Vercel Plugin on Claude Code wants to read all your prompts!

item.title: Native Instant Space Switching on MacOS
item.description: Arhan's Blog

item.title: The Token-Burn Trap: Silicon Valley's AI Metric Is a Competitive Liability
item.description: Token spend is not a productivity metric. It's an effort metric. And effort is not outcome.

item.title: The Old Internet is Still Here
item.description: The “old” Internet isn’t gone, our expectations have changed.

item.title: What To Do With Your Extra Tokens
item.description: Surplus AI coding tokens are wasted capacity. Use them for targeted code reviews, UI audits, product analysis, and marketing - with copy-paste prompts for each.
item.author: (John Higgins)

item.title: Giving LLMs a Formal Reasoning Engine for Code Analysis
item.description: Dmitri's blog about programming, Clojure, and software development

item.title: Code Is Cheap Now, And That Changes Everything | Pere Villega
item.description: AI coding agents have made code production nearly free. Drawing on insights from Kent Beck, Paul Ford, and Simon Willison, this post argues that the value has shifted from writing code to defining systems — contracts, invariants, SLAs, and verification.

item.title: Do Science in Bed
item.description: On April 8, 2026, I had a conversation with Claude (Anthropic) that went from a loose intuition about dark matter to a testable astrophysics prediction in peer-reviewable form, in one sitting. From my phone.

item.title: An homage to doing – kbrecordzz
item.description:
The deadliest thing of all isn't to do bad things, it's to do nothing at all. If you do wrong you can always learn from it and redirect yourself, but if you do nothing you won't ever get to that point. In this reality, you can either do things or not do things, and it's 100% up to you. And if you want to make it easier for yourself to do things, it seems to come down to two things:
- Doing what you intrinsically enjoy
- Having a greater purpose

item.title: Code is not abstracted away - Ahmet Hüseyin Dok
item.description: and why you should still read the code

item.title: What Are You Trying to Say?
item.description: Sometimes, I find myself talking while my audience has a puzzled look on their face. It doesn't matter how much I prepared my speech, the message is just not getting through. But then they ask: What a

item.title: Anatomy of a change
item.description:
Exactly a year ago today, I changed something about myself that had been part of my life for ten years.
What that "thing" is won’t be mentioned here, so a...

item.title: Stabilizing Benchmarks
item.description: Ways to stabilize benchmark results, to reduce noise.

item.title: Pipeline Architecture System Design
item.description: 🧵 Most People Overcomplicate This System Design Question

item.title: The Slow Startup Movement | Wisdom | Tarikh Korula
item.description:
What if, instead of speeding everything up, AI let us build intentional businesses?
We're all so busy arguing about whether AI will replace programmers, kill creative jobs, or consolidate power into fewer and fewer companies. I think there might be a quieter possibility nobody's talking about, one far more radical and less dystopian than any of those arguments.
I had a conversation with a former client, and fantastic CTO who, after some initial skepticism, has fully drunk the AI Koolaid. We
item.author: (Tarikh Korula)

item.title: Clean code in the age of coding agents
item.description: Keeping code clean helps both humans and agents. Coding agents are limited by context, and poorly organized code means they consume more tokens and perform worse. Here are some practical steps to keep your code clean for both humans and agents.

item.title: The Patterns That Agents Miss
item.description: We built a synthetic clinical dataset with known ground-truth patterns and fed it to 18 automated data analysis tools. Most of them confidently reported incorrect results.

item.title: The Scroll Manifesto
item.description:
Scroll inside the stage with the mouse wheel or with your finger. It is only a machine, but it already understands the gesture. Have fun!

item.title: My Friend Circles
item.description: I was clearing my desktop recently when I came across a graphic I made couple of years ago to describe people in my life to another friend. How I interpret different people in my life. Looking back at it couple of years later I found it interesting, looking back at the people who came and went in my life.

item.title: How NASA Built Artemis II’s Fault-Tolerant Computer – Communications of the ACM
item.description: The computer system aboard the current Artemis II lunar space mission is from a different world that the one from the Apollo era. Apollo astronauts navigated to the lunar surface using a computer with a 1-MHz processor and roughly 4 kilobytes of erasable memory, supported by a larger store of fixed “rope” memory. While it was a marvel of 1960s engineering, the Apollo Guidance Computer’s functional scope was focused and not in the control loop for every system. Critical environmental and power controls were managed through manual or electromechanical means, such as switches and relays.

item.title: EFF is Leaving X
item.description: After almost twenty years on the platform, EFF is logging off of X. This isn’t a decision we made lightly, but it might be overdue.

item.title: Help Keep Thunderbird Alive!
item.description: Thunderbird is a free email application that’s easy to set up and customize - and it’s loaded with great features!

item.title: Claude mixes up who said what, and that's not OK
item.description: Claude sometimes sends messages to itself and then thinks those messages come from the user. This is categorically distinct from hallucinations or missing permissions.

item.title: Open source security at Astral
item.description: Insights and guidance from our engineering team on how Astral secures its tools.

item.title: John Deere to Pay $99 Million in Monumental Right-to-Repair Settlement
item.description: The ag manufacturing giant will also make digital diagnostic, maintenance, and repair tools available to third parties for 10 years.

item.title: USB for Software Developers | WerWolv
item.description: A basic introduction to USB for people that don't need to know what happens on the wire
